notes.note: Marcus Aemilius Lepidus was a distant connection of Brutus through aristocratic family ties; Cicero sardonically notes that Lepidus ranks as nearest relation those he most hates (i.e., the Liberators).
Publius Servilius Vatia Isauricus the younger (consul 48 and 41 BC), a consular senator who leaned toward Antony; Cicero had to endure his obstruction in the Senate while trying to honor Plancus.

notes.anchor: on the fifth day before the Ides of April
notes.note: Ante diem quintum Idus Apriles = 9 April 43 BC.
Publius Cornelius Lentulus Spinther, a Republican commander operating in the East alongside Cassius; his letter brought news of Cassius's seizure of Syria and the Syrian legions.

notes.anchor: about Cassius, about the legions, about Syria
notes.note: Cassius Longinus had taken control of Syria and the legions stationed there in early 43 BC, a major coup for the Liberators against the Antonian faction.

notes.anchor: a great prodigy in the commonwealth
notes.note: The Latin monstrum carries a strong religious sense — a portent or unnatural sign; Cicero treats Servilius's political conduct as a bad omen for the Republic. The sentence breaks off (indicated by the triple asterisk *** in the manuscripts).
